speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six. We're interviewing Judith Velencei. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were talking about what made you decide to come to the United States. You left the-- your family in Hungary. And how was life in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before we got to the United States, I had to find my husband. And it wasn't easy, because such a mess of Hungarian refugees concentrated in Austria. The Red Cross never registered anybody. So you couldn't go through any agency to try to find anybody. The only first registration happened in the different consulates when you-- once you decided you want to come someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't have much chance or much hope I will get reunited with him very easily. But faith was working for me. And one-- the second day I-- I arrived to the first camp, which was in Salzburg, in Austria, and I went to have breakfast in a cafeteria, we bumped into each other. I opened the door, and he was just coming out. And that's how we reunited in Austria, with my husband. How many-- can I go back? How many-- how long were you separated from him before you decided to join him?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: He left three weeks before--

speaker: interviewee
text: He left.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he already registered to the United States. That was the only place he ever wanted to go, and that was the last place I wanted to go. I had a wrong picture in me about United State, and I begged him to change his mind. I wanted to stay in Europe, first of all, to be least that much closer to my family. Then, my second choice, Israel, my third choice, Australia, but not the United State. But how lucky I am I came to the United [LAUGHS] State, because we established ourselves very quickly. My husband was in a automobile business as a mechanic, and later he bought his own business. And within 10 years he was a car dealer. And we lived very successfully and very well. And I don't know how much you want me to go into life in America, but it was difficult for me to go home the first time, because I was away for so long.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first visit was almost 10 years after I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in 1966.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nine years. Yes. And it-- it was hard. But I was determined to come back, because I wanted to be sure I see my father before too late. He had a very difficult health problem with his heart and hardening of the artery, and I was concerned I could lose him any time. And I wanted to spend some time with him. And I was lucky, because I was able to. And we had many, many long talks, and he explained a lot of things which I did not understood when I was a child. And we never talked about it after the liberation. And this time, we were free to discuss anti-Semitism or anything which I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you-- when you look back at your experiences during the war, what was it that helped you to survive the Shoah, you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, first of all, because I was a very strong individual, because of the liberty, the way my parents raised me. And I was very self-confident. Many time happened from boarding school when I had to travel alone. I was able to confront grownups without trouble. I was very strong and very determined. And my love for my family and to see them again, I think it gave me enough to survive anything, much more what I had to go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could-- could you have survived without the help of non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way. No way, especially the time and effort my Catholic grandmother and all three aunt put in. Without their help, and without their information, they were all key people in our survival. One of them quit the job just to able to travel with my brother from place to place, so he wouldn't have to go to the police station to register. The one who worked in a mayor office, who got the most updated bulletin before it's even got on the radio. The third one who worked in a post office, who was-- which was-- was also a telephone center. And many time she overheard conversations which was not for her ear, but if somebody was calling from a high places, she tuned in. And if it was an information which was important for the family, she made sure we get that information. And all this good friends, the priest in town, [INAUDIBLE], on the other town, and all the people who helped us in our way, I don't think without a trusted friend, we could had done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you feel that, in any way, your father's economic situation helped you a great deal to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely. He must had a tremendous amount of money saved up which were not in banks, because they took the bank money. They put their hand on it. They put--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The government.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Germans occupied Hungary, he could not touch his money if he wouldn't had it in a illegal place. And I know from the lifestyle we lived in hiding, it must have cost a fortune. Those traveling, that was not cheap. I remember my little pocketbook, and my mother opened up the seams and filled it up with paper money. I don't know how much money I had in there. She never said so. She only told me, that is an awful lot of money in there. Don't ever open up, only when your life is in danger. But I knew I can bribe people. And I was willing to do that if I had to. But without money, no. A lot of people, as good they are, but to risking their life without any reason, no, I don't think there is too many of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you go back now, how do you feel about the Hungarians and their attitude towards their-- the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Hungary is luckily a far more sophisticated or more liberal, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like Poland is. I don't think we have as much anti-Semitism as some other Eastern European countries has. And personally, I didn't find it-- I never talk to people who against Jewish people. And now, in this late time, when there is very few of the survivor are still [LAUGHS] survived, I think there is even a great sympathy toward anybody Jewish. I noticed my sister, who was raised Catholic, after came back to my home, she's proud to be Jewish, and she practically advertise it. The first thing she would tell anybody, I just came back from Israel to visit my father. And-- and-- and look, everything Jewish. She-- she liked to go to Jewish places. She's very much-- it's almost like-- it's a in thing to be Jewish in Hungary, least in a country. And when I go to visit into my birthplace, Tibolddaroc, where the whole town still know me, they greet me with such a friendliness and with admiration. It boosts my spirit every time I go to visit that village. So I am one of the lucky one. I don't say it might be everybody's experience. Probably not. But I can't complain. I was always surrounded with love, and I think that's what make me a happy person. Even in the worst time, I had people sticking up for me. And that's why I am never got depressed or never been bitter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I just consider myself very lucky. Before the end-- we end the-- this interview, I'd like to ask you a-- another question, which-- when-- when you came to the United States and you settled with your husband and your family, what was-- what did you do as an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five years after we came here, when my son started to go to school full day, I decided to look for a job. I wanted to get out of the house. I'm not cut out just for be a housewife. And I was looking for a office job, which was very difficult. I didn't know that time, but it was an-- a serious unemploymancy in the beginning of the '60s. And I had to go to an agency to ask them, and tell them I'm willing to pay for it, but find me a job. I want an office job. And I want some place that is close enough so I can commute. And so on, so on. The same day, they send me out for an interview. I got the job, and I am still with the same company now about 34 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the company is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Hooper Holmes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am a supervisor of a big billing department. Our billing, monthly, is about $15 million. So it is a big company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back, again, at your experiences, you remember-- you-- your recollection of what happened to you is very, very strong. Do you have any idea of why you remembered so much, or what gave you the ability to remember so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: I consider it my duty to remember. I think I read it someplace the Jewish people have a-- in their faith, in their faith saying everybody die only after nobody remember them. But as long as somebody recall, [SOBBING] somebody remember you, you are alive. And I think of my family, [SOB] my cute little nieces and cousins, and I almost feel like a duty to remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And even so, I didn't talk about it for a very long time, because I just felt too bad about it to talk, and because a lot of people don't wanted to listen, or at least not with the intenseness I wanted them to listen. I shut up. But by no mean, mean to forget. And when my son got big enough, I started to talk to him and tell him stories. And without my encouragement, he became very proud of his Jewish heritage. And he always was interested in that life. And I'd bring him books from the Holocaust, and he loved to read them. And we'd discuss it afterwards, and so on, so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, one last question. Is there any message you would like to give to future generations about the Shoah or the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever your faith is, Jewish or Catholic, [SOBBING] please be more tolerant, because everything can be solved in time. But we all need time. And my heart is breaking when I hear the news from Israel and Palestine. I sympathize with the Palestine people, too. They need a place they own, too. And my heart goes out for the Israelis. And there is no reason why they cannot live side by side. If both would honor the other and understand the other, there is no reason why [SOBBING] it cannot be. And there's nothing greater than peace and love. That's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judith, the Shoah Foundation would like to thank you for sharing your testimony with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to introduce Judith Velencei. And would you please introduce the man sitting next to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd be glad to. This is Thomas Velencei, but he like to be called Tom.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's my only child. Tom, could you tell us how you-- your mother's wartime experiences has affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's made me understand [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or given me reason to be more tolerant of others. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After hearing a lot of things and kindnesses that were given to my mom for no apparent reason, and she follows through on that in her life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I wish I could do more. And every time I hear her experiences, and-- it reinforces the reasons why we should all have more tolerances in this world, of others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- did you find, growing up as a child, different from what other children experienced as growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's-- that's a different story. I wasn't aware of her experiences until my early adulthood. So as a child, I-- I had no idea of any of her experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judith, why is it that it wasn't until your son was a young adult that you spoke to him about your experiences during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't talk about it at home. After the war, and after we realized nobody will return, and that's all what we have left, we tried to make ourself a new life. And it was just so ugly. I think it was my father who really was hesitant to talk about it. He never did anything, he never said anything, any indication, even to his own Jewishness, only the fact he was very generous when it came to some Jewish cause. I know he was a great contributor to a statue which came up in a-- a Jewish cemetery. And all the donations which came to him and he was always very generous in it, but he never looked for Jewish organizations or even to Jewish friends. He was always very much content within his family. And we don't talked about it. My mother couldn't face it anymore. My mother completely broke down when it was after. When everything was smooth and everything was just fine, that's when my mother broke down. And I heard her saying once, if anybody would say she had to go through the whole thing, she would have the-- he would-- she would be strong enough to kill her family and kill herself, but she would not allow that to happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So under that kind of a atmosphere, we just not talked about it. And I didn't know if Tommy would understand it, especially here in this country. So it was when he got a little bit big when-- when the first time, I said, well, your grandfather was in concentration camp. And he said, really? And little by little, the story started to come out. And I felt his attitude is changing. And he didn't realize it was a new world opening up for him. And how do you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- that-- it was culture shock. It was just amazing what I heard, some of these stories that to me came out of nowhere, you know. And some of the incidents in-- in our family, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] chance things of-- of people in our family walking thousands of miles looking for the-- for the other one and finding them. Just our family was very well blessed in so many ways and in what had happened during those times that it's just-- it's not even real. Even though it was very bad, it-- it-- it-- it was very good in-- in many ways that just-- just amazes me. And it was through the kindness of-- of many strangers, for-- for no apparent reason or for-- many of them did it for-- for no reward. And just something to think about all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other message you want to give to your son and to his future family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he knows I am very proud of him, and I'm very happy he got interested and involved in our past. And he's not only involved in the past, but he's very involved in the present. He keep very close contact with my stepfather's children who live in Israel. He considered them cousins. And he would go to visit Israel before he would go to visit Hungary. And he did that a couple of time. And he always feel very much at home there. And he loved the people of Israel. He loved their lifestyle. He's-- he's very happy every time he go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I am very happy he turned out the way he turned out, without my guidance, practically, but just from the little stories I dropped off here and there to him to hear. And that's-- it's a great influence. But I just know he will keep on the same course.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know he's going to be just as tolerant and I am, which basically we are. And I learned it from my father. And I'm passing it on to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, both of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Mrs. Ignac Schvarcz. The picture is taken about 1943 [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I forgot to watch that. The back of the picture served as an identification for my mother during the Holocaust. They gave her this so she can prove her mother and father, they were both were Catholic. This picture was taken after a school play in our little village of Tibolddaroc. My father-- show a very proud father of two children there. The picture was taken in Hungary, I think about 1937 or '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Please tell us the names of the people in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is Ignac Schvarcz. My brother, Otto; and me, Judith. This is the picture of the Schvarcz family. Standing in the back is my father, Ignac Schvarcz. To the right is my mom, Jozefa. And me standing in the center, Judith. And next to me, my brother, Otto. The picture was taken about in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary. This is the picture of my three aunt, which called the Klima sisters. Left to right, first one is Elza, the youngest. In the middle is Ilona. And the right is Martha.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture was taken, I believe, in 1943, in Tibolddaroc, Hungary. I think that wasn't-- this is the picture of me, Judith Velencei. The picture was taken in the middle of the worst time of my life in 1944, about in July, in the city of Gyomro. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The name on-- on my dress is a B, because I used my nickname, Botsy. This house, in front of that is my great-uncle and aunt and a family friend. The picture was taken very long time ago, perhaps by the time I was born, in Hungary, Miskolc. It is only interesting for me because that's the only picture I have which shows the place where the cave was. Underneath that house that was a wine cellar. And under the wine cellar, where my uncle Antal digged the cave for us to say-- stay during the Holocaust. This is the house located in Tibolddaroc, Hungary. The picture was taken during one of my visit around 1992. This is the location and part of the housing compound we had. The grandmother's house is torn down, but this building, which you can see in the front, it was part of our home and is still standing. This is the power station my father established in 1932. The picture was taken in my-- one of my visit in 1992. And today, it is completely changed, but it used to be a power station and also an apartment when my grandmother with his-- with her two daughter lived in Hungary. This picture was taken in 1986, in Tibolddaroc, Hungary, during my first visit back to my birth place. The place is entirely different as it used to be. They were wine cellars, but most of them is no more operating. This was-- this picture was taken in 1966 in Miskolc, Hungary. That was the family home during-- after the-- after the war. And that's where we went with my son for the visit the first time to Hungary. [BACKGROUND NOISE]
